subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child uses tools to identify, describe, and draw special four-sided shapes and sorts flat shapes based on their features.
Your child can tell the difference between a physical map that shows land and water and a political map that shows countries and borders.
Your child can observe and describe shapes by their important features, sort and draw them, and begin learning about fractions by dividing shapes.
Your child uses tools to explore the features of triangles and circles.
Your child can locate all the continents on a map or globe.
Your child understands how Gestalt ideas help us see and understand things as whole patterns.
Your child can find important lines on maps and globes like the prime meridian, equator, Tropics of Capricorn and Cancer, International Date Line, and lines of latitude and longitude.
Your child learns about shapes by looking at their parts and how they fit together, and practices dividing shapes to understand fractions.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Math: Geometry — Classifying and Analyzing Shapes
coherence_reason: The standards cluster both geometry concepts (shapes, fractions, classification) and map/geography concepts (maps, continents, lines of latitude/longitude), which are clearly distinct topics.
split_topics: Geometry: Classifying, Describing, and Dividing Shapes
Geography: Map Types, Continents, and Map Features

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can describe the world using maps and other geography tools.
Your child can explain how industrial growth, city life, new communication, and cultural changes affected life in the U.S. from the late 1800s to World War I.
Your child can explain how artifacts and archaeological finds help us learn about prehistoric people and how they moved.
Your child understands how regions help organize and describe Earth's surface.
Your child can analyze early civilizations by looking at their technology, jobs, government, calendars, and writing.
Your child can describe how people worked to improve society and change unfair conditions during the Progressive Era.
Your child understands why Europeans explored and settled in Alabama and how this affected trade, health, and land.
Your child can name important events and celebrations that happen in your town and in Alabama.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Social Studies: Historical Developments — Geography, Artifacts, and Social Change
coherence_reason: The standards span multiple topics, including geography tools, historical analysis, archaeology, regional organization, and both local and national events, with little direct conceptual overlap.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child compares a fictional story about a time or place with a real historical account to see how fiction changes history.
Your child compares fictional stories to real historical accounts to understand how authors change history in their writing.
Your child can compare a made-up time, place, or character in a story to the real version in a history text.
Your child can use calendars and timelines to learn about events from the past.
Your child can compare a fictional story about a time or place with a real historical or cultural account to see how authors change history in fiction.
Your child compares stories and historical facts to understand how fiction changes real events.
Your child compares a fictional story about a time or place with real historical facts to see how authors change history in stories.
Your child compares stories with real history to see how authors change or use facts in fiction.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Literary Texts — Comparing Fiction and Historical Accounts
coherence_reason: Most standards emphasize comparing fictional stories and real historical accounts, but one standard about calendars and timelines is slightly outside the main concept.
split_topics: Using calendars and timelines to learn about history

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child understands how saving money and investing money are connected and how they work together.
Your child can use math skills to figure out the cost of borrowing money.
Your child learns ways to protect their income and savings.
Your child can compare the good and bad points of different credit products and services.
Your child looks at how people choose to spend money based on what they have and what they need or want.
Your child can use different ways to build wealth and increase what they own.
Your child understands the services that banks and financial institutions offer.
Your child thinks about how people’s feelings and habits about money, saving, investing, and work affect their relationships.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Financial Literacy — Personal Finance Concepts
coherence_reason: All standards are related to personal finance, though they mix practical financial skills with socio-emotional and decision-making aspects.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child understands how to use all information sources, like the internet and AI, honestly and responsibly.
Accountability and Ethics
Personal Responsibility
Your child can describe what might happen if they share certain personal information with others.
Your child can use ways to protect their personal information from being shared without permission.
Your child knows why it’s important to keep some personal information private and safe.
Well-being
Rights & Responsibilities
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Digital Citizenship — Personal Information and Responsibility
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on responsible use and protection of personal information, though some reference broader concepts of ethics and well-being.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child compares a written story, play, or poem to its audio or video version to understand how each uses different techniques.
Your child compares a text with its audio, video, or multimedia version to see how each shows the subject differently.
Your child can compare a written story, play, or poem to an audio, video, or live version of the same text.
Your child can compare a written text to an audio, video, or multimedia version of the same text.
Your child can look at digital and mixed-media texts and explain their features.
Your child can compare a written story, play, or poem to its audio, video, or live version and explain the differences.
Your child can compare a written text to its audio, video, or multimedia version and explain how each way of sharing the story affects its meaning.
Your child compares a written text with audio, video, or digital versions to understand how each format shows the subject differently.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Literature — Comparing Written and Multimedia Versions
coherence_reason: All standards focus on comparing written texts to their audio, video, or multimedia versions to analyze differences in presentation or meaning.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child uses technology like the internet to write and share their ideas clearly.
Your child uses technology like the Internet to write, publish, and share work, including citing sources and working with others.
Your child can use technology like the Internet to write, publish, and work with others.
Your child can use technology, like the Internet, to write and work with others.
Technology
Your child can share their writing with the right people who will read it.
Your child can use technology like the Internet to write and share their work clearly and effectively.
Your child can use technology and the Internet to write, publish, and work with others.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Writing — Using Technology to Produce and Share Writing
coherence_reason: All standards focus on students using technology to write, publish, and share their work, with some mentioning collaboration or citing sources, but all under the umbrella of using technology for writing production and distribution.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child finds useful information from books and the internet, checks if the sources are trustworthy, and shares the information correctly without copying.
Your child finds information from different sources, checks if it’s trustworthy, and uses it correctly without copying, following citation rules.
Your child can find quotes with useful information about a topic from different books or online sources.
Your child can gather information from books and online sources, check if the information is trustworthy, and use it without copying.
Your child can show where they found information and use sources honestly.
Your child knows the difference between original sources and summaries or reports about them.
Your child knows the steps needed to do a good experiment and make sure the results are trustworthy.
Your child can find and choose important information from many different sources.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Research — Gathering and Crediting Information
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on gathering, evaluating, and crediting sources, but one standard about experimental procedures is only loosely related.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can do research using more than one source to answer a question.
Your child will do short and longer research projects by asking questions and showing they understand the topic.
Your child does short research projects using several sources and asks new questions to learn more.
Your child does short research projects to answer questions, using several sources and asking new related questions to learn more.
Career Research
Your child can do short research projects to answer questions, using several sources and asking more focused questions to explore.
Your child can do both short and longer research projects by focusing on questions and showing they understand the topic.
Researching
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Research — Conducting Projects and Using Multiple Sources
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on conducting research projects using multiple sources and generating questions, but 'Career Research' and 'Researching' are vague and could refer to broader or different subtopics.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child places phrases and clauses correctly in sentences and fixes misplaced or unclear modifiers.
Your child can identify which words need capital letters and choose the right punctuation at the end of a sentence.
Your child explains what phrases and clauses do in sentences.
Your child can tell if a sentence is a statement or a question.
Your child writes complete sentences that match subjects and verbs and avoids sentence mistakes like run-ons or fragments.
Your child understands how prepositions and phrases affect how verbs match their subjects.
pronoun-antecedent agreement
Your child understands what phrases and clauses do in sentences and can explain their roles.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Grammar and Sentence Structure — Phrases, Clauses, and Sentence Clarity
coherence_reason: The standards cover related topics in grammar and sentence structure, but they address distinct sub-concepts such as modifier placement, capitalization/punctuation, sentence types, subject-verb agreement, and pronoun-antecedent agreement.
split_topics: Sentence boundaries and types (run-ons, fragments, statements/questions, capitalization, punctuation)

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child chooses different sentence types to show how ideas are connected.
Your child can use words like 'because' and pairs like 'either/or' to make complex sentences.
Your child can choose different types of sentences to show how ideas are connected.
Your child can write different types of sentences—simple, compound, complex, and compound-complex—to show how ideas are connected.
Your child chooses different types of sentences to show how ideas are connected.
Your child can form verbs by adding -ing, -ed, or -s to show different times or amounts.
Your child can use different verb forms that show ongoing, completed, or ongoing completed actions.
Your child uses different sentence types and keeps verb tenses consistent to combine ideas clearly in writing.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Sentences — Types, Connections, and Verb Tense
coherence_reason: The cluster covers both sentence types/structure for connecting ideas and the use and formation of verb tenses, which are related but distinct subtopics.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can take part in group discussions by being prepared, following rules, staying on topic, and listening to others.
Your child participates well in different types of group discussions, listens to others, and shares their own ideas clearly.
Your child follows rules for group discussions, keeps track of goals and deadlines, and understands their role in the group.
Your child follows detailed steps carefully when doing experiments or technical tasks.
Your child can join different kinds of group talks about grade 7 topics, listen to others, and share their own ideas clearly.
Your child asks questions to get more information and responds to others with helpful comments to keep the discussion on track.
Your child gets ready for and takes part in conversations with different people, building on others’ ideas and sharing their own clearly and confidently.
Your child listens to new information from others and changes their own ideas when it makes sense.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Speaking and Listening — Collaborative Discussions
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on participating in group discussions, listening, and sharing ideas, but one standard about following steps in experiments or technical tasks is off-topic.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can find a word that means the opposite of another word in a text.
Your child uses dictionaries, glossaries, or thesauruses, in print or online, to find how to say a word, what it means, or its part of speech.
Your child will ask for help or look for clues when they find a word they don't know while reading or talking.
Your child checks the meaning of a word or phrase by looking at how it’s used or by using a dictionary.
Your child can check the meaning of a word or phrase by looking at the context or using a dictionary.
Your child can use dictionaries, glossaries, and thesauruses, both print and online, to find how to say a word, its meaning, part of speech, or history.
Your child uses dictionaries, glossaries, or thesauruses, in print or online, to find how to say a word, what it means, or what part of speech it is.
Your child can use dictionaries, glossaries, or thesauruses, in print or online, to find how to say a word, what it means, or what part of speech it is.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Vocabulary Acquisition — Using Reference Materials
coherence_reason: Most standards address using reference materials to determine word meanings, but one standard about identifying antonyms and another about using context clues introduce minor variation.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can read and understand nonfiction texts for grades 6–8, with help if needed for harder parts.
Your child can read and understand literary nonfiction for grades 6–8, with help if needed for harder parts.
Your child understands the meaning of special words and symbols used in science or technical texts.
Your child can answer simple questions about basic informational texts like schedules, calendars, or directories.
Your child can read and understand history and social studies texts for grades 6-8 on their own.
Your child can read and understand science and technical texts for grades 6-8 on their own.
Your child can read and understand difficult stories and information on their own.
Your child shows they understand what they read or hear in nonfiction books and articles.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Reading — Informational Text Comprehension
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on comprehension of nonfiction and informational texts, though some emphasize specific domains (science/technical) and skills (vocabulary, answering questions) which are related but slightly distinct.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can write regularly for different reasons and for different people.
Your child can write regularly over short and long periods for different tasks and audiences, including time to research and revise.
Your child writes regularly over short and long periods for different subjects, purposes, and audiences, including time for research and revising.
Your child writes regularly over short and long periods for different subjects, purposes, and readers, taking time to think and improve their work.
Your child writes regularly over short and long periods for different subjects, purposes, and readers.
Your child can write regularly over short and long times for different school subjects, purposes, and audiences.
Your child can write regularly over long and short times for different tasks, purposes, and audiences, including time for research and revising.
Your child can write regularly over short and long periods for different subjects and audiences.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Writing — Regular Practice for Varied Purposes and Audiences
coherence_reason: All standards focus on regular writing over varying timeframes for different tasks, purposes, and audiences, with minor wording differences only.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can understand and judge information presented in different ways, like pictures, numbers, and words.
Your child combines and evaluates information from different sources like pictures, numbers, and spoken words.
Your child combines information written in words with pictures like charts or graphs to understand it better.
Your child can pick out details from information they hear or see in different media.
Your child can match information to the correct heading or part in a chart or text.
Your child uses pictures, charts, or maps along with written information to understand a topic better.
Your child can find what is the same about two characters when shown a picture or chart to help.
Your child can combine different pieces of information to come up with new ideas or understandings.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Integrating and Evaluating Information — Multiple Media and Formats
coherence_reason: All standards focus on integrating, evaluating, or comprehending information presented through multiple media formats such as text, images, charts, and spoken words.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child introduces a topic clearly and organizes information using methods like comparison or cause and effect, including helpful headings or visuals.
Your child introduces a topic clearly, organizes ideas into groups, and uses headings, charts, or pictures to help readers understand.
Your child can introduce a topic, share ideas and information about it (using pictures or other media if needed), and use topic-specific words in their writing.
Your child introduces a topic clearly, organizes ideas into categories, and uses headings, charts, or multimedia to help understanding.
Your child introduces a topic clearly, organizes ideas using methods like comparison or cause and effect, and uses headings or visuals to help understanding.
Your child starts their writing by clearly introducing the topic and organizing ideas in a logical way.
Your child clearly introduces the topic and previews what will be discussed.
Your child organizes ideas using methods like comparing or explaining causes and effects, uses transitions to connect ideas, and ends with a clear conclusion.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Informational Writing — Introduce and Organize Ideas
coherence_reason: All standards focus on introducing a topic clearly and organizing information logically in informational writing, often mentioning the use of headings, visuals, and organizational methods like comparison or cause/effect.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can pick or make audio recordings and pictures to help make a point during a presentation.
Your child adds pictures, videos, or other media to presentations to help explain ideas and highlight important points.
Your child can add pictures, videos, or other media to presentations to help explain their ideas clearly.
Your child uses digital tools and visuals to share information and make presentations easier to understand.
Your child can share their findings by writing, speaking, or using different ways like videos or slides.
Your child can make daily schedules, calendars, and timelines to organize events.
Your child includes pictures, videos, or other media in presentations to make their points clearer and more interesting.
Your child can add different types of digital media to make their speaking or writing flow better and be more connected.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Presentation Skills — Incorporating Digital Media
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on adding digital media to presentations, but some address organizing events with schedules and timelines or sharing information in various formats, which are distinct subtopics.
split_topics: Organizing and sharing information with schedules, timelines, and varied formats

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child uses commas correctly between adjectives that equally describe a noun, like in 'a fascinating, enjoyable movie.'
Your child can use the correct punctuation at the end of a sentence or question.
Your child can use commas and semicolons correctly to make their writing clear.
conjunctive adverbs
Your child can use commas correctly to separate adjectives that equally describe a noun.
Your child uses punctuation like commas, ellipses, and dashes to show pauses or breaks in writing.
Your child uses punctuation such as commas, parentheses, dashes, and hyphens to make writing clearer and better.
Your child can use commas correctly to separate two or more adjectives that describe the same noun.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Conventions — Punctuation Usage
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on correct use of punctuation, particularly commas, but a few also mention other punctuation marks and broader concepts, with a minor emphasis on conjunctive adverbs.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can explain how the author's point of view or purpose affects what is written and how it is written.
Your child can figure out the author’s purpose or point of view in a text.
Your child can pick out words that show how the author feels about the topic or guess why the author wrote the text.
Your child analyzes how an author shows different characters' or narrators' points of view.
Your child notices parts of a text that show the author's opinion or purpose, like strong words or missing facts.
Your child figures out the author's point of view and how they show it is different from others.
Your child can tell what the author thinks or why they wrote an informational text.
Your child can compare how two or more characters or narrators see things in a story.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Reading Comprehension — Author's Purpose and Point of View
coherence_reason: Most standards address analyzing author's purpose or point of view, but some also include comparing characters' or narrators' points of view, which is a related but distinct sub-skill.
split_topics: Comparing characters' or narrators' points of view

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can explain the meaning of figures of speech, like references to stories or myths, based on the context.
Your child understands how words are related and how to use them.
Your child understands figurative language, how words relate to each other, and the subtle differences in word meanings.
Your child shows they understand what they read or hear in stories, plays, or poems.
Your child understands figurative language, how words relate to each other, and subtle differences in word meanings.
Your child knows what words mean.
Your child understands both the exact and creative meanings of words when reading.
Your child understands figurative language, word relationships, and subtle differences in word meanings.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Vocabulary — Figurative Language and Word Relationships
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on figurative language and word relationships, but a few are broader about general word meaning and comprehension.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can match a word or phrase with a picture that shows how a person or character feels in a story.
Your child can figure out what simple sayings or expressions mean when reading a story.
Your child finds the meaning of words and phrases in a text, including figurative language, and notices how sounds like rhymes affect a poem or story.
Your child can find and use words that show how people or characters feel in a story or text.
Your child understands the meaning of words and phrases used in history and social studies texts.
Your child can tell the difference between words that have similar meanings but different feelings or associations.
Your child can tell how certain words or phrases are used to convince or inform the reader in a text.
Your child can find words or phrases that describe how people or characters feel in a story.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Vocabulary and Word Meaning — Figurative Language and Connotation
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on interpreting word meanings, figurative language, and connotation, but there is a minor emphasis difference between literary and informational texts.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can figure out the meaning of familiar words when they see them with matching pictures.
Your child uses clues from the sentence or paragraph to understand the meaning of a word or phrase.
Your child can use clues in a sentence to pick the word or words that fit best.
Your child can use clues from the story and pictures or objects to figure out what familiar words mean.
Your child can pick the correct meaning of a word or phrase when given choices with pictures or objects to help.
Your child can figure out the meaning of new or tricky words by using different helpful strategies.
Your child uses relationships between words, like synonyms and antonyms, to better understand their meanings.
Your child uses what they know about word parts and sentence structure to understand new words in harder reading.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Vocabulary — Context Clues and Word Relationships
coherence_reason: All standards relate to determining word meanings using context clues, word relationships, and structural analysis in reading.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can use common root words and add beginnings or endings to make new words, like 'like,' 'liked,' or 'disliked.'
Your child uses common Greek or Latin word parts to help figure out the meaning of new words.
Your child can use common Greek or Latin roots and prefixes to help figure out the meaning of words.
Your child can figure out the meanings of words by knowing common Greek and Latin roots and word parts.
Your child can figure out the meaning of words by knowing common Greek and Latin roots and word parts, with help.
Your child uses common Greek or Latin prefixes and roots to help figure out the meaning of new words.
Your child uses common Greek or Latin word parts to help figure out what new words mean.
Your child builds new words by using Greek and Latin roots and prefixes or suffixes and uses those words correctly in sentences.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Vocabulary — Greek and Latin Roots, Prefixes, and Suffixes
coherence_reason: All standards focus on using Greek and Latin roots, prefixes, and suffixes to determine and build the meaning of words.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child explains a speaker’s argument and checks if the reasons and evidence make sense and support the claims.
Your child can decide if information from a speaker is a fact or just an opinion.
Your child can tell if something a speaker says is a fact.
Your child can explain a speaker's argument and decide if the reasons and evidence make sense.
Your child follows and evaluates an author's argument to see if the reasons and evidence make sense.
Your child can find and think about the main points and reasons in a text and decide if the evidence is strong and relevant.
Your child can tell the difference between facts, opinions, and thoughtful judgments in a text.
Your child thinks about a speaker’s opinion, reasons, and evidence to decide how strong their argument is.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Argumentation — Evaluating Claims, Reasons, and Evidence
coherence_reason: Most standards address evaluating arguments in both spoken and written texts, with minor variations in focus between fact/opinion identification and assessing the strength of arguments.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can find, list, or organize facts and details that support a topic.
Your child adds facts, definitions, details, and examples to explain the topic well.
Your child can write to share information and include facts or details about the topic.
Your child can pick out a fact or detail that matches a given topic.
Your child adds facts, definitions, details, and examples to explain their topic.
Your child can choose facts or details that are connected to a certain topic.
Your child can share what they have learned about a topic and include important facts or details.
Your child develops a topic using well-chosen facts, definitions, details, quotes, or examples.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Informative Writing — Developing a Topic with Relevant Facts and Details
coherence_reason: All standards focus on identifying and using relevant facts and details to develop a topic in informative writing.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can choose the right word to use when given a list of words that are often mixed up.
Your child learns and uses new academic and topic-specific words correctly, especially words important for understanding or expressing ideas.
Your child can use general and subject-specific words in different situations.
Your child will learn and use new academic and subject-specific words correctly to help understand and express ideas.
Your child learns and uses a wide range of academic and subject-specific words for reading, writing, speaking, and listening, and can find out new word meanings on their own.
Your child learns and uses a wide range of general and subject-specific words for reading, writing, speaking, and listening, and figures out new words on their own.
Your child can learn and use new academic and subject-specific words correctly to understand and express ideas better.
Your child will use grade-appropriate academic words when speaking and writing.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Vocabulary — Academic and Domain-Specific Word Acquisition and Use
coherence_reason: All standards focus on acquiring, understanding, and properly using academic and subject-specific vocabulary in various contexts.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child will write about events or personal experiences.

Your child can give details about a person, character, or action that happened during an event or experience when preparing to write or talk about it.

Your child will write stories about real or imagined events using good details and clear order.

Your child can give a detail about a person or character, often with a picture, when getting ready to write or talk about an event or experience.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Narrative Writing — Personal and Imagined Events
coherence_reason: All standards focus on writing narratives about real or imagined events with attention to detail, character, and event order.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can get ready for formal talks and use good speaking skills when presenting.

Your child presents ideas clearly and focuses on important points using facts, details, and examples, while speaking with good eye contact, volume, and pronunciation.

Your child shares information and evidence clearly so listeners can follow the ideas and the style fits the purpose and audience.

coherence_reason: All standards focus on preparing and delivering clear, organized, and effective formal presentations tailored to audience and purpose, using appropriate speaking skills.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can use language to communicate well with both peers and adults, such as asking questions, giving more information, or making requests.

Your child can use language to get their message across when talking or writing.

coherence_reason: All standards focus on students’ ability to use language appropriately and correctly in both speaking and writing, tailored to audience and context.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: With some help, your child improves their writing by planning, revising, editing, or trying new ways to make sure it fits the purpose and audience.

With some help, your child improves their writing by planning, revising, editing, or trying new ways to better reach their audience.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can tell how two texts about the same topic are alike and different in the details they share.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child writes a conclusion that supports the information or explanation they shared.
Your child writes a conclusion that supports the information or explanation they gave.

Your child writes a conclusion that supports the argument they presented.
Your child writes a conclusion that supports and follows the information or explanation they gave.
Your child writes a conclusion that supports and follows from the information or explanation they gave.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Writing — Composing Conclusions for Arguments, Explanations, and Narratives
coherence_reason: The standards all address writing conclusions, but for three distinct writing types: arguments, explanations/information, and narratives/experiences.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child uses different transition words to show the order of events and changes in time or place.
Your child uses exact words and subject-specific vocabulary to explain a topic clearly.
Your child chooses words that clearly and briefly express ideas, avoiding extra or repeated words.
Your child uses transition words to connect ideas and make their writing easy to follow.
Your child can write about a topic or text by making a claim, giving reasons to support it, and using words like 'first' or 'next' to connect ideas.
Your child uses clear and specific words related to the topic to explain or inform.
Your child uses words and phrases to connect claims, counterclaims, reasons, and evidence clearly.
Your child uses exact words and detailed descriptions to show actions and share experiences.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Writing — Word Choice and Transitions
coherence_reason: All standards relate to effective writing through word choice and transitions, though some focus on vocabulary precision, others on logical connections.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can find details that support the main idea when listening to or watching something.
Your child can pick out details in a story that support the main idea or message.
Your child can find one or more main ideas in an informational text.
Your child can understand main ideas and details from different types of media and explain how they help explain a topic.
Your child can find the main ideas or themes in a text, explain how they develop, and summarize the important details.
Your child can find one detail that supports the main idea in a short informational text when the main idea is given.
Your child can tell you the main idea or message of a short story.
Your child finds the main ideas or conclusions in a text and summarizes them clearly without adding personal opinions.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Reading Comprehension — Main Idea and Supporting Details
coherence_reason: All standards focus on identifying main ideas and supporting details in various texts and media, with consistent emphasis across descriptions.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can use information from stories or facts from nonfiction to support their writing, like telling the difference between real and made-up characters or using evidence to back up their ideas.
Your child uses specific examples from history or social studies texts to support their understanding.
Your child can use details from a short informational text to answer simple who, what, where, or when questions.
Your child uses specific examples from the text to support their understanding of science and technical topics.
Your child uses facts and information from nonfiction texts to support their thinking and research.
Your child uses evidence from stories or informational texts to support their thinking, reflection, and research.
Your child comes to discussions prepared by reading or researching and uses evidence to explore and think about ideas.
Your child will find and use evidence from stories or information texts to support their thinking and research.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Reading — Using Textual Evidence
coherence_reason: All standards focus on students using evidence or specific details from various texts (literary and informational) to support their thinking, writing, understanding, or research.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can tell when information is clearly stated in a text and when they need to figure it out from clues.
Your child can answer questions about a story by using clear details and clues to figure things out.
Your child uses several pieces of evidence from the text to explain what it says and what can be inferred.
Your child can use details from a short informational text to answer questions and make guesses about what might happen.
Your child can tell the difference between facts, opinions based on research, and guesses in a text.
Your child can use clues from an informational text to answer questions and make smart guesses (inferences).
Your child can read carefully to understand what a text says and make logical guesses, using examples from the text to support their ideas.
Your child can find information that is clearly stated in a story and also figure out things that are not directly said.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Reading Comprehension — Citing Evidence and Making Inferences
coherence_reason: All standards focus on using textual evidence to distinguish between explicit statements and inferences in both literary and informational texts.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can tell how an informational text is organized and explain how a fact or event fits into that structure.
Your child looks at how the author organizes the text and how each part helps explain the topic.
Your child can explain how a reason or claim fits into the way an informational text is organized.
Your child can recognize different types of informational texts and how they are organized, like by order, cause and effect, or comparing things.
Your child explains how a text organizes information, like in order, by comparison, or by cause and effect.
Your child looks at how the form or structure of a play or poem helps explain its meaning.
Your child looks at how an author organizes a text and how each part helps explain the ideas.
Your child can look at how sentences and paragraphs fit together in a text and explain how they help the whole story or article.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Informational Text — Text Structure and Organization
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on informational text structure and how parts contribute to ideas, but one includes plays and poems, which is a minor variation.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can explain how different parts of a story, such as characters, setting, or events, are related.
Your child can understand how different parts of a story work together to make the whole story meaningful.
Your child can match one part of a story, like a character or setting, with another related part.
Your child can figure out how two people, events, or ideas in a text are related.
Your child analyzes how parts of a story or play, like setting and characters, work together.
Your child can explain how people, events, and ideas change and connect throughout a story or text.
Your child figures out why the author explains something, describes a process, or talks about an experiment.
Your child can explain how two people, events, or ideas in an informational text are connected.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Text Structure — Relationships Among Elements
coherence_reason: The standards address relationships among elements across both literary (characters, setting, events) and informational texts (people, ideas, events, author's explanations), which are related but distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can write informative texts that explain complex ideas clearly by choosing and organizing information well.
Your child writes informative texts that explain events, scientific steps, or technical processes.
Your child can tell why an informational text was written, like to give directions or share information, using words and pictures.
Your child writes informative texts that explain a topic by choosing and organizing important facts and ideas.
Your child can write informative texts that explain complex ideas clearly by choosing and organizing important content.
Your child writes informative texts that explain a topic by choosing and organizing important information.
Your child can write informative texts that explain history events, science experiments, or technical steps.
Your child can write clear explanations using information from several sources, including good details and a logical order.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Writing — Informative/Explanatory Texts
coherence_reason: All standards focus on writing informative or explanatory texts, emphasizing clarity, organization, and explanation of complex ideas or processes.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child can write in a way that fits the task, purpose, or audience.
Your child can write clearly and organize their ideas so the writing fits the purpose and audience.
Your child writes clearly and in an organized way that fits the task, purpose, and audience.
Your child can write clearly and organized for the purpose and audience they are writing for.
Your child writes clearly and in an organized way that fits the task, purpose, and readers.
Your child can write clearly and organized in a way that fits the task, purpose, and audience.
Your child can write clearly and in an organized way that fits the task, purpose, and audience.
Communicating Through Writing
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Writing — Clarity, Organization, and Audience Appropriateness
coherence_reason: All standards focus on writing clearly and in an organized way that fits the task, purpose, and audience.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child supports their claims with clear reasons and accurate facts from trustworthy sources.
Your child supports their claims with clear reasons and evidence from trustworthy sources.
Your child will write arguments that support their claims with good reasons and enough evidence.
Your child introduces their claim about a topic, recognizes other views, and organizes reasons and evidence logically.
Your child introduces their claim, mentions opposing views, and organizes reasons and evidence logically.
Your child writes arguments focused on specific subjects.
Your child writes arguments that support their claims with clear reasons and evidence.
Your child will write arguments that clearly explain their opinions with good reasons and supporting facts.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Writing — Argumentative Claims and Supporting Evidence
coherence_reason: All standards focus on constructing written arguments with clear claims, logical organization, and supporting evidence or reasons.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child keeps a formal tone when writing informational texts.
Your child writes in a formal and objective way.
Your child keeps a formal style when writing arguments.
Your child writes in a formal style.
Your child can write letters or emails that share opinions, complaints, or questions in a polite and clear way.
Your child writes in a formal style and keeps an objective tone.
Your child writes using a formal style when appropriate.
Your child writes in a formal style when needed.
canonical_name: Grade 7 Language Arts: Writing — Formal Style and Tone
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on using a formal style and tone in writing, though one includes polite letter/email writing, which is slightly broader.
